title: Inventory Errors & Their Ripple
event: Reyes Co. miscounted ending inventory this year by $4,000. Determine the effect on this year's cost of goods sold and net income, and on next year.

computationPaths.narration: COGS = Goods available − ending inventory. Inflate the ending count and COGS shrinks, so income inflates. Next year inherits the error in reverse — it self-corrects (counterbalances) over two years.
Understate the ending count and COGS inflates, so income deflates — then next year flips it back.

memorize.body: COGS = Beginning inventory + Purchases − Ending inventory. Ending inventory and COGS always move in OPPOSITE directions.
This year's ending inventory is next year's beginning — that's why the error flips sign and washes out over two years.

questions.prompt: Ending inventory overstated by $4,000. This year's net income is ______ by ?

distractors.feedback: You flipped the direction. Overstated ending inventory → understated COGS → OVERSTATED income by 4,000.
The error doesn't double within one year — it's 4,000 this year, reversing 4,000 next year.
